Article.ArticleTitle: Uniaxial stretching of nucleotide-based fibers upon dehydration yields aligned and single-stranded form with the same interbase distance as B-DNA without adopting A-DNA.

Abstract.AbstractText: Significant research over the past few decades, particularly using single-molecule manipulation techniques, has provided a comprehensive understanding of how mechanical tension alter DNA's mechanics. Meanwhile, the effect of tension on the DNA structure under dry and wet conditions has not been elucidated well so far. In this study, we produced regenerated DNA-based fibers derived from salmon milt by solution spinning and investigated the structural changes under dry and wet condition during uniaxial stretching using synchrotron X-ray scattering. As a result, the DNA molecules adopted randomly disordered single-stranded structure and did not show structural changes until break under dry condition. On the other hand, DNA fibers were not subjected to tension and presented randomly disordered B-form structure during the initial stage of stretching under wet condition. As the stretching proceeds, the DNA fibers started to be tensioned along with the water removal and presented improved molecular alignment, while maintaining the same base pair distance as B-DNA. Further stretching and water removal did not adopt A-DNA but yielded single-stranded, uniaxially oriented conformation with the same interbase distance as B-DNA. The obtained result was in stark contrast to the structural change of DNA without tension, in which the conformation changes from B- to A-form and finally transforms into a single-stranded disordered structure as the water content decreases. This research will contribute to understanding of fundamental biophysics of the hydration-force landscape of DNA molecules and bio-inspired design to apply DNA as structural and functional materials in a bulk scale.
